steps.title: Vader, Moeder, Kind 👨‍👩‍👧
steps.desc: Family is a huge topic in Dutch small talk. Let's learn the closest family members and their articles.
goals: De vs het with family words

steps.example: Mijn vader werkt bij een bank.
steps.exampleEn: My father works at a bank.
steps.note: A de-word. Informally: 'papa' or 'pap'. Most Dutch people say 'papa' at home.
A de-word. Informally: 'mama' or 'mam'. Dutch families are often close-knit.
A het-word! But the plural 'de kinderen' takes 'de' — all plurals do! This catches many learners off guard.

steps.example: Mijn broer is ouder dan ik.
steps.exampleEn: My brother is older than me.
steps.note: A de-word. 'Grote broer' = big brother, 'kleine broer' = little brother.
A de-word. Plural: 'zussen'. 'Grote zus' = big sister. Also formal: 'de zuster'.
Always plural (de-word). 'Ouder' also means 'older' — same word! Context makes it clear.

steps.title: 💡 Het kind → De kinderen
steps.text:
This is one of the trickiest things in Dutch articles:

• het kind (the child) — neuter, het-word
• de kinderen (the children) — plural, always de!

All plurals in Dutch take 'de', even if the singular is a het-word:
• het huis → de huizen
• het boek → de boeken
• het kind → de kinderen

So you only need to memorize de/het for singular nouns!

steps.q: What article do ALL plurals get?
steps.hint: Even het-words become de in plural!

steps.title: Hebben — Irregular!
steps.text:
'Hebben' (to have) doesn't follow the regular pattern.
You need to memorize it:

ik heb
jij hebt
hij/zij heeft
wij hebben
jullie hebben
zij hebben

⚠️ In questions with jij: 'Heb jij...?' (drops the -t)

Full conjugation → Grammar tab → A1 → Zijn & Hebben

steps.title: Opa & Oma 👴👵
steps.desc: Meet the extended family! Grandparents, uncles, aunts — and learn the possessive pronouns.

steps.title: Possessive Pronouns
steps.text:
Quick reference — you'll need these for family:

mijn — my
jouw / je — your (informal)
uw — your (formal)
zijn — his
haar — her
ons / onze — our (ons + het-word, onze + de-word)
hun — their

⚠️ Only ons/onze changes based on de/het!

Full explanation → Grammar tab → A1 → Possessive Pronouns

steps.note: A de-word. Also: 'de grootvader' (formal). Most Dutch people say 'opa' — it's warm and affectionate.
A de-word. Also: 'de grootmoeder' (formal). 'Oma's koekjes' = Grandma's cookies — the 's shows possession!
A de-word. Fun fact: the Dutch word for 'uncle' sounds exactly like 'ohm' — the electrical unit!
A de-word. Dutch people often use 'oom' and 'tante' even for close family friends — a sign of affection.

steps.exampleEn: My son is five years old.
steps.note: A de-word. Sounds similar to English 'son'! Plural: 'zonen' or 'zoons'.
A de-word. Related to English 'daughter' — both from the same ancient root.

steps.title: 👤 Possessive pronouns
steps.text:
Here are all the possessives you need:

🔹 mijn — my
🔹 jouw / je — your (informal)
🔹 uw — your (formal)
🔹 zijn — his
🔹 haar — her
🔹 ons / onze — our
🔹 hun — their

⚠️ Special rule for 'ons/onze':
• ons + het-word: ons huis (our house)
• onze + de-word: onze auto (our car)
• onze + plural: onze kinderen (our children)

This is the ONLY possessive that changes based on gender!

deepDive.title: Why 'zijn' and 'haar' can be confusing
deepDive.text:
'Zijn' means both 'his' AND 'to be':
• Zijn vader is arts. = His father is a doctor.
• Where 'zijn' = his AND = is!

'Haar' means both 'her' AND 'hair':
• Haar haar is blond. = Her hair is blond.

Context always makes it clear, but it trips up beginners!

steps.q: Which possessive changes based on de/het?

steps.title: Big or Small? 📸
steps.desc: How many brothers do you have? Is your family big? Let's learn to describe families with adjectives and 'hebben'.

steps.note: A het-word! 'Gezin' = nuclear family (parents + children). 'Familie' = extended family. This distinction is very Dutch!
A de-word. Borrowed from English! Diminutive: 'het babytje' — with het because all diminutives are het-words.
Remember adjective endings! 'Een groot gezin' (een + het-word = no -e), but 'het grote gezin' (het-word + definite = -e).
Same adjective rule: 'een klein huis' (een + het = no -e), but 'de kleine tuin' (de-word = always -e).

steps.title: 💬 Talking about your family
steps.text:
Common family questions & answers:

❓ Heb je broers of zussen?
— Do you have brothers or sisters?
💬 Ik heb twee broers en een zus.
— I have two brothers and one sister.

❓ Hoe groot is je gezin?
— How big is your family?
💬 We zijn met z'n vijven.
— There are five of us.

❓ Heb je kinderen?
— Do you have children?
💬 Ja, ik heb een zoon en een dochter.
— Yes, I have a son and a daughter.

steps.q: What is the difference between 'gezin' and 'familie'?
steps.hint: Gezin is parents + children at home

steps.title: Family Reunion 🎉
steps.desc: Practice round! Combine all family vocabulary, possessives, and descriptions in real conversations.

steps.title: 📸 Describe this family
steps.text:
Practice describing a family:

Dit is mijn gezin. — This is my family.
Mijn vader heet Pieter en mijn moeder heet Sanne. — My father is called Pieter and my mother is called Sanne.
Ik heb een broer — hij heet Lars. — I have a brother — he's called Lars.
Mijn opa en oma wonen in Den Haag. — My grandpa and grandma live in The Hague.
Wij zijn met z'n vijven. — There are five of us.
Ik houd van mijn familie! — I love my family!

'Ik houd van' = I love (for people and things)

steps.q: 'Ons huis' vs 'Onze auto' — why the difference?
opts: Huis is het-word (ons), auto is de-word (onze)
Huis is small, auto is big
steps.ans: Huis is het-word (ons), auto is de-word (onze)
steps.hint: Ons/onze changes based on de or het!

steps.q: How do you say 'I love my family'?
